request.question: What is the setting for the game engine Snowdrop?

title: List of game engine recreations
Game engine
Id Tech 4
Unreal Engine
Rockstar Advanced Game Engine
Amazon Lumberyard
Snowdrop (game engine)
Game engine recreation
Tom Clancy's The Division
Zero (game engine)
sentences: Game engine recreation is a denomination for game engines that are clones of the original game engine binary that came with the video games.
The main motivation behind such a project is the wish to play the game on other operating systems than those supported by the original game engine.
A game engine is a software framework designed for the creation and development of video games. Developers use them to create games for consoles, mobile devices and personal computers.

The core functionality typically provided by a game engine includes a rendering engine ("renderer") for 2D or 3D graphics, a physics engine or collision detection (and collision response), sound, scripting, animation, artificial intelligence, networking, streaming, memory management, threading, localization support, scene graph, and may include video support for cinematics.
The process of game development is often economized, in large part, by reusing/adapting the same game engine to create different games, or to make it easier to port games to multiple platforms.
id Tech 4, popularly known as the "Doom 3" engine, is a game engine developed by id Software and first used in the video game "Doom 3".
The engine was designed by John Carmack.
Carmack also created previous game engines, such as those for Doom and Quake, which are widely recognized as significant advances in the field.
This OpenGL-based game engine has also been used in "Quake 4", "Prey", "", "Wolfenstein", and "Brink".
The Unreal Engine is a game engine developed by Epic Games, first showcased in the 1998 first-person shooter game "Unreal".

Although primarily developed for first-person shooters, it has been successfully used in a variety of other genres, including stealth, MMORPGs, and other RPGs. With its code written in C++, the Unreal Engine features a high degree of portability and is a tool used by many game developers today.
It has won several awards, including the Guinness World Records award for "most successful video game engine."
The Rockstar Advanced Game Engine (RAGE) is a proprietary game engine developed by the RAGE Technology Group at Rockstar San Diego, with contributions by other Rockstar Games subsidiaries.
The engine has been used on several different platforms such as Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 3, PlayStation 4, Wii, Xbox 360 and Xbox One.
The Angel Game Engine was originally developed by Angel Studios for "Midtown Madness" and later the sixth generation console era versions of the "Midnight Club" series and other Angel Studios games.
Amazon Lumberyard is a free cross-platform triple-A game engine developed by Amazon and based on the architecture of CryEngine, which was licensed from Crytek in 2015.
The engine features integration with Amazon Web Services to allow developers to build or host their games on Amazon's servers, as well as support for livestreaming via Twitch.

Additionally, the engine includes Twitch ChatPlay, where viewers of the Twitch stream can influence the game through the associated chat, a method of play inspired by the "Twitch Plays Pokémon" phenomenon.
The source code is available to end users with limitations: users may not publicly release the Lumberyard engine source code or use it to release their own game engine.

Lumberyard launched on February 9, 2016 alongside GameLift, a fee-based managed service for deploying and hosting multiplayer games, intended to allow developers to easily develop games that attract "large and vibrant communities of fans."

s of February 2017 , the software is currently in beta and can be used to build games for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4, Xbox One, with limited support for iOS and Android, and support for Linux and Mac coming as well.
Virtual reality integration was added in Beta 1.3, allowing developers to build games supporting devices like Oculus Rift and HTC Vive.
Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.
It was first revealed at E3 2013 with "Tom Clancy's The Division", the first game using the engine.
Game engine recreations are remade engine interpreters for video games that replace the original engine binary that came with the original game.

A notable example of game engine recreation is ScummVM which successfully recreated the SCUMM engine of classical LucasArts' point and click adventures. For further examples, refer to the list of game engine recreations.

Tom Clancy's The Division is an online-only action role-playing video game developed by Massive Entertainment and published by Ubisoft, with assistance from Red Storm Entertainment, for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4 and Xbox One.
It was announced during Ubisoft's E3 2013 press conference, and was released worldwide on 8 March 2016.

It is set in a near future New York City in the aftermath of a smallpox pandemic; the player, who is an agent of the eponymous Strategic Homeland Division, commonly referred to as simply "The Division", is tasked with helping the group rebuild its operations in Manhattan, investigate the nature of the outbreak, and combating criminal activity in its wake.
"The Division" is structured with elements of role-playing games, as well as collaborative and player versus player online multiplayer.
Zero is a proprietary game engine created by Pandemic Studios.
It was used first in the game "" and later used in several "Star Wars" games including the popular "Battlefront" series.
"Battlezone II: Combat Commander" and "Dark Reign 2" both feature an in-engine editor accessible via commands.
The engine was revamped for "" to accommodate consoles and third person gameplay.
The engine was again retooled for "" and the level editor was made a separate entity from the game engine.
A set of modding tools including ZeroEdit, the new level creation tool, were released for use with "Star Wars: Battlefront" on December 23, 2004.
An updated version of the tools were released on February 2, 2006 for "".
Pandemic used Zero as their primary engine for several of their games developed in their Los Angeles, California studio.
A modified version of the Zero engine was also used in Pandemic's "Mercenaries 2: World in Flames".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: What is the setting for the game engine Snowdrop?
hop1_docs: Snowdrop (game engine) | Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.  It was first revealed at E3 2013 with "Tom Clancy's The Division", the first game using the engine.

Information set (game theory) | In game theory, an information set is a set that, for a particular player, establishes all the possible moves that could have taken place in the game so far, given what that player has observed.  If the game has perfect information, every information set contains only one member, namely the point actually reached at that stage of the game.  Otherwise, it is the case that some players cannot be sure exactly what has taken place so far in the game and what their position is.

List of snowdrop gardens | Snowdrops (species of "Galanthus") are popular late winter or early spring flowers which are celebrated as a sign of spring and can attract large numbers of visitors to places where they are growing.  The reason for their popularity is that snowdrops can form impressive carpets of white in areas where they are native or have been naturalised.  Most 'Snowdrop Gardens' will have the common snowdrop, "Galanthus nivalis", but some have more unusual snowdrops, some which may be unique to the garden that they are growing in.  The rarest may only survive in that garden due to the conditions and environment.
HMS Snowdrop | Two ships of the Royal Navy have borne the name HMS "Snowdrop" after the flower, the Snowdrop:

Galanthus nivalis | Galanthus nivalis, the snowdrop or common snowdrop, is the best-known and most widespread of the 20 species in its genus, "Galanthus".  Snowdrops are among the first bulbs to bloom in spring and can form impressive carpets of white in areas where they are native or have been naturalised.  They should not be confused with the snowflakes, in the genera "Leucojum" and "Acis".

USS Snowdrop (1863) | USS "Snowdrop", originally named "Albert DeGroat" and occasionally referred to as "A. DeGroat" was a screw tug built in 1863 at Buffalo, New York.  "Albert DeGroat" was purchased by the United States Navy at New York City on 16 October 1863; renamed "Snowdrop"; and was fitted out at the New York Navy Yard.
HMS Snowdrop (1915) | HMS "Snowdrop" was an "Azalea"-class sloop of the Royal Navy.
response.summary_1:
Snowdrop (game engine)

- Facts from passages: "Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft"; it was "first revealed at E3 2013".
- Platforms stated: "Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One".
- First game: "Tom Clancy's The Division" — described as "the first game using the engine".
- Ambiguity / missing info & next hops (prioritized): "setting" is ambiguous (could mean engine runtime platforms or a game's in-universe setting). Bridge entities: check "Snowdrop (game engine)" — lede/infobox and "Platforms"/"Supported platforms" for the platform interpretation; check "Tom Clancy's The Division" — lede/infobox and "Plot"/"Setting"/"Synopsis" sections for the game's in-universe setting.
response.hop2_query: Snowdrop Tom Clancys The Division setting
hop2_docs: Snowdrop (game engine) | Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One.  It was first revealed at E3 2013 with "Tom Clancy's The Division", the first game using the engine.

Tom Clancy's Net Force | Tom Clancy's Net Force is a novel series, created by Tom Clancy and Steve Pieczenik and originally written by Steve Perry.  The original series ceased publication in 2006.  Relaunched in 2013, and currently written by veteran Tom Clancy author Jerome Preisler, it is set in 2018 and charts the actions of Net Force: a special executive branch of the United States government set up to combat increasing crime and terrorist activity on the Internet.  There was also a spin-off of young adult books called "Net Force Explorers".

Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Jungle Storm | Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Jungle Storm is an expansion of "Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon" released on the PlayStation 2.  It contains the content of "" along with eight new single-player missions set in Colombia and additional multiplayer maps.

Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six: Shadow Vanguard | Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six: Shadow Vanguard is a 2011 first-person shooter developed and published by Gameloft for iOS, Xperia Play and Android devices.  The game is set within the continuity of "Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six" media franchise.

Tom Clancy's The Division | Tom Clancy's The Division is an online-only action role-playing video game developed by Massive Entertainment and published by Ubisoft, with assistance from Red Storm Entertainment, for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4 and Xbox One.  It was announced during Ubisoft's E3 2013 press conference, and was released worldwide on 8 March 2016.  It is set in a near future New York City in the aftermath of a smallpox pandemic; the player, who is an agent of the eponymous Strategic Homeland Division, commonly referred to as simply "The Division", is tasked with helping the group rebuild its operations in Manhattan, investigate the nature of the outbreak, and combating criminal activity in its wake.  "The Division" is structured with elements of role-playing games, as well as collaborative and player versus player online multiplayer.

Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Future Soldier | Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Future Soldier is a third-person tactical shooter video game developed and published by Ubisoft for the PlayStation 3, Xbox 360 and Microsoft Windows.  It was released in May and June 2012.  "Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Future Soldier" was announced to be in development by Ubisoft on January 22, 2009.  The game has a futuristic take on the "Ghost Recon" series.  The campaign has settings such as Nigeria, Pakistan, Russia, and Norway.

Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell: Double Agent | Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell: Double Agent is an action-adventure stealth video game, developed and published by Ubisoft.  The series, endorsed by American author Tom Clancy, follows the character Sam Fisher, an agent employed by a black-ops division of the National Security Agency, dubbed Third Echelon.  "Double Agent" was released for the GameCube, PlayStation 2, Xbox and Xbox 360 in October 2006.  The Wii and Microsoft Windows versions were released in November 2006.  A PlayStation 3 version was released in March 2007.  Originally the game was set for a March 2006 release, but Ubisoft moved the release date to October 2006 in order to have more development time.  Ubisoft then released their fiscal quarter results for Q1 2006 and announced that "Double Agent" would be put back at least one month in order to boost Q3 2006 income.
hop2_titles: Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Jungle Storm
Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six: Shadow Vanguard
Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Future Soldier
Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell: Double Agent
response.summary_2: Snowdrop is a proprietary game engine created by Ubisoft for use on Microsoft Windows, Nintendo Switch, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One. Tom Clancy's The Division is set in a near-future New York City in the aftermath of a smallpox pandemic. Tom Clancy's The Division was the first game to use the Snowdrop engine.
response.answer: a near-future New York City in the aftermath of a smallpox pandemic
